translation.content: The RA Podcast launched 19 years ago in 2006, making it one of the world's longest-running online mix series. As we gear up to celebrate the 1,000th episode, RA.999 lands with five mixes across five days with a cohort of artists who've left a singular mark on electronic music over the past few decades. The chemistry and legacy of these duos make for something very special—and worthy of such a huge milestone. First up was 1morning and Regal86 with a live recording from New York's Bossa Nova. Next up? We move to NYC Downlow, and who better to capture Glastonbury's beloved queer utopia than Prosumer and Peach, going back-to-back for the first time. The best house lives in the body—it oozes right down into your core, coils through your spine and invites your body to swing and roll from the hips in a way that only this music can. This mix is an homage to classic house in all its moods, rich with groove, colour and warmth. Think deep, sultry rhythms, soulful vocals, luminous synths and ornamental claps, toots, beeps and whistles. If house is your thing, then Prosumer and Peach going one-for-one on the decks is nothing short of a treat. With Glastonbury still just about in the rearview mirror, this mix captures the vibrant spirit of the Downlow. If you're familiar, you'll know this means steamy euphoria all-night long, sweat dripping from the ceiling and an intoxicating feeling in the humid air. And Prosumer is a true Downlow darling–a treasured custodian and storyteller of dance music who delivered his first RA Podcast way back in 2007. "If a record doesn't move me emotionally or physically, I won't play it out," he said at the time, befitting of the impeccable curation and irresistible body groove that became his trademarks. He is, in the words of the Johnny Dangerous track in the mix, the "King of Clubs." For Peach, RA.999 marks a full-circle moment: Prosumer's 2019 closing set in the Downlow is one of her all-time Glastonbury memories. Since her first RA Podcast in 2021, the London-based artist has only grown more in-demand, with sets that typically traverse the house and techno lexicon—and occasionally R&B—with a distinctly peppy energy. "Neither of us are afraid to go deep," she said about playing with Prosumer. "We just had fun with it." So strap in, or rather, don't—get up and let your body move. After all, you know what they say, house is a feeling, and it runs through both of these artists' veins. - Katie Thomas How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? Prosumer: The mix was recorded at RA HQ the Friday after Glastonbury with decks and CDJs. Peach and myself felt like smooth, sexy house vibes. Peach: There was a moment where I almost took a gig in Ibiza that would've meant we needed to record on the Wednesday right after Glastonbury–which would've been a lot. But Prosumer was really understanding and so thoughtful, and suggested we go for a spa day before the mix to chill me out. Thank god the gig didn't work out, I definitely needed that time to settle my nerves and recover. Other than the spa day, the only thing we really discussed was that I'd play "House Arrest" by Krush at some point. What have you been up to recently? Prosumer: I've just started providing vocals for a project. We're not sure if it's ever going to be released, but it's fun. Let's see where it leads. I'm having a great summer, quite busy with gigs but also getting to relax in nice places in between. I've been to Croatia, Provincetown, New York, Los Angeles and San Francisco recently. Peach: Mostly doing my best to keep out of trouble during festival season. Since we recorded this, I've been to Butik, Love International and a friend's wedding in Portugal. In between at home, I've been working on my new project Dreamland and doing a few remixes, as well as finalising an EP and mixing down another–both of which will (hopefully) come out later this year. This is the first time you've played with each other, how did you prepare for the mix together, and how do you think you complement each other? Prosumer: The main thing for me was not to make too many plans, but to be open to feeling inspired by Peach's choices. While recording, we communicated about where we were going to take things. We're both quite playful people, which I think works well together. Peach: Besides the obvious interest in house, there are a few threads between the music we both play. For example, neither of us are afraid to go deep, and we can keep it energetic yet stripped back. I think you can hear that in this dynamic, and we just had fun with it. To be completely honest, I spent the weeks leading up to this freaking out to any of my friends that would listen. I'm a huge Prosumer fan, and one of my favourite Glastonbury memories was seeing him close the Downlow in 2019. There was a song from that set that a friend and I have been obsessed with ever since, and he ended up playing it in our back-to-back! A very full-circle moment. Your first RA Podcasts came out in 2007 and 2021 respectively. If you could summarise how the dance music landscape has changed in the time since, what would you say? Prosumer: 18 years! That's crazy. I guess the usual cycle of trends has been going on–things being recycled, reclaimed and rediscovered. I think the main change has been how money is being distributed. Big names over residents, festivals over clubs. People prefer to spend tons of money on a week at a festival instead of having a regular relationship with nightlife and clubs at the weekend. Peach: I think when I recorded my RA Podcast we were still between lockdowns. I remember playing it to a room of friends in Dublin on New Year's Day, when there was still a lot of uncertainty around what was next. Obviously clubland has since resumed, the touring DJ schedule is back more than ever and festivals seemed to go through difficult times initially but have since started recovering and doing even better than before. At the time, there was a lot of hopeful discourse around what people could do to improve dance music in a post-pandemic world. But since then, it feels like everyone has forgotten about that and gone back to where we were before the pandemic–specifically in terms of more interesting bookings that aren't just focused on big headliners, and support for local scenes. Which dance floor has had the most impact on you as an artist, and why? Peach: Maybe it's unsurprising, but the first place that comes to mind is the NYC Downlow. I've had many nights in there, but one specifically sticks from three years ago that really made me reassess how and what I DJ. I came away from it recognising and knowing that, regardless of all the music I love and respect, playing house has and always will feel the most true to me. We recently wrote that your set in the NYC Downlow each year has become something of a "rite of passage." How would you describe the club, and how special it is, to someone unfamiliar? Prosumer: Oh god, that's like asking to describe love to someone who's never felt it, or sex to someone who has never had it! Glastonbury in itself is pretty magical, it feels like decades of people gathering to celebrate and be joyful has left a special energy. The Downlow is a part of that, you just feel the love in the air. I've seen so many of my colleagues play outstanding sets there. It's definitely a place of inspiration, something that helps all of us to do our best. It's sweaty as fuck and you better not wear much. Or something fabulous. What do you hope for in the rest of 2025, musically or otherwise? Prosumer: I hope we'll get better at witnessing the state of the world without having to despair over it or become uncompassionate in order to deal with it. We can't afford to disconnect. We have to hope and support each other as fellow humans. And we're not our governments. Music wise: have a little fun, be a nerd, be yourself. If you're trying to be cool, you're not. Peach: Honestly, it can feel increasingly depressing to look at the state of the world between governments failing us and modern genocides being live-streamed. It's easy to feel helpless at this time and I often do. I just really hope we keep supporting one another throughout this instead of attacking one another, confiding and speaking with those close to us about our feelings regarding it all and doing what we can within our reach to help.
translation.blurb: Part two of RA.999: two NYC Downlow favourites go back-to-back for the first time with a jubilant homage to classic house.
